description: Deployment-ready JSON bundle for LLM-assisted auditing of mathematical papers: (A) Knuth-style math writing rules, (B) IEEE/ACM citation rules, and (C) theorem/proof rigor checks.

embedded.title: Knuth Principles for Mathematical Writing (LLM Audit Rules)
embedded.description: Practical Knuth-style rules for mathematical prose: sentence starts, adjacent formulas, punctuation with displays, symbol introduction and definition, consistent notation, and reader guidance.

provenance.basis: User-provided distilled Knuth principles attributed to Donald E. Knuth's mathematical writing guidance.

rules.title: Do not start sentences with bare symbols
rules.rationale: Bare symbolic openings are hard to read and sound abrupt in mathematical prose.
rules.check: Flag sentences that begin with math tokens, variables, quantifiers (∀,∃), operators, or displayed math unless the symbol is part of a proper noun or numbered item.
rules.pass_criteria: The sentence begins with ordinary language that names or describes the mathematical object.
exceptions: Enumerated algorithmic pseudocode lines rather than prose sentences.
Display-equation lines that are not sentence starts in the surrounding prose.
good_examples: The function f(x) is continuous on [0,1].
For every x in X, there exists y in Y.
rules.fix_template: Rewrite the sentence so it begins with words naming the object or quantifier domain.

rules.title: Do not place two formulas adjacent without words between
rules.rationale: Adjacent formulas without connective words are difficult to parse and often ambiguous.
rules.check: Flag occurrences of consecutive inline math segments (e.g., $f(x)$ $g(x)$) or symbol clusters where a conjunction/preposition is expected.
rules.pass_criteria: Between any two formulas that refer to distinct objects, there are connector words (and, or, implies, such that, of, etc.).
exceptions: Explicit multiplication or function composition expressed in a single formula.
Tuple/vector notation inside one formula.
good_examples: Consider f(x) and g(x).
Let A and B be sets.
rules.fix_template: Insert a connective word (e.g., “and”, “of”, “with”, “such that”) between the formulas.

rules.title: Displayed equations must be grammatically integrated and punctuated
rules.rationale: Displayed equations are part of the sentence; missing punctuation breaks grammar and reader flow.
rules.check: Flag displayed equations that are followed by no punctuation when the surrounding prose implies continuation, or that use incorrect punctuation (e.g., trailing colon after verb 'is/are').
rules.pass_criteria: Displayed equations end with appropriate punctuation (comma/period/semicolon) matching the sentence structure.
exceptions: Standalone display that is not part of a sentence (rare; should be avoided).
Numbered equation displayed as a label-only line in some templates (still discouraged).
bad_examples:
We define
  x = y + 1
where y > 0
good_examples:
We define
  x = y + 1,
where y > 0.

Then
  f(x)=0,
and hence ...
rules.fix_template: Add punctuation to the displayed equation (typically a comma or period) so the paragraph reads as a single grammatical sentence.

rules.title: Define symbols before (or very near) first use
rules.rationale: Undefined notation causes misinterpretation and makes proofs unverifiable.
rules.check: Flag any symbol that appears without a prior definition in the same section or a clearly stated standing assumptions/notation block.
rules.pass_criteria: Every nonstandard symbol is defined at first appearance (or immediately before) with domain/type information if relevant.
exceptions: Truly standard constants (0, 1, π, e) and common sets (ℝ, ℕ) if the venue allows unexpanded use.
Globally defined notation in a dedicated Notation section.
bad_examples: Let δ = min(δ1, δ2). (δ1, δ2 never defined.)
good_examples: Let δ1, δ2 > 0 be given by continuity of f and g at a, and set δ = min(δ1, δ2).
Let X0 denote the set of feasible solutions defined in (2.1).
rules.fix_template: Insert a definition sentence/phrase for the symbol (and its domain) at its first use or move the first use to after the definition.

rules.title: Use consistent notation and do not reuse symbols for different meanings
rules.rationale: Symbol reuse breaks logical correctness and proof traceability.
rules.check: Flag cases where the same symbol is reintroduced with a new meaning, or where multiple symbols denote the same object without explanation.
rules.pass_criteria: Each symbol has exactly one meaning in its scope, and each object has one primary notation (with declared synonyms if needed).
exceptions: Standard dummy indices in summations/integrals with clearly local scope.
Overloaded notation explicitly declared as such (discouraged).
bad_examples: Let G be a graph. ... Let G be a group.
Let n be the sample size. ... Let n be the normal vector.
good_examples: Let G be a graph. ... Let H be a group.
Let n be the sample size. ... Let ν be the normal vector.
rules.fix_template: Rename one of the meanings (choose a new symbol) and propagate consistently through the relevant scope.

rules.title: Avoid long unconnected sequences of formulas
rules.rationale: Listing formulas without explanatory prose resembles homework and obscures the argument.
rules.check: Flag paragraphs with multiple consecutive sentences that are primarily formulas or equation transformations with no explanatory link words.
rules.pass_criteria: Formulas are tied together by prose explaining the goal and the reason for each transformation.
exceptions: A short chain of equalities/inequalities with an explicit stated reason.
Algorithmic derivations where each line is annotated by a justification.
good_examples: Starting from x=..., we substitute into the definition of y to obtain y=.... Finally, applying Lemma 2 yields z=....
rules.fix_template: Insert short prose between formulas indicating the reason (definition, substitution, lemma) and the intermediate goal.

rules.title: Prefer words over nonstandard symbolic logic in running text
rules.rationale: Symbols like ∀, ∃, ⇒ are easy to misread in prose and reduce accessibility.
rules.check: Flag ∀, ∃, ⇒, ⇔, s.t., w.r.t., iff in prose sentences outside formal logic contexts.
rules.pass_criteria: Quantifiers and implications are written in words when embedded in prose.
exceptions: Works explicitly about formal logic where symbols are the subject matter.
Short summary tables of logical forms (not prose).
good_examples: For every ε>0 there exists δ>0 such that ...
We show that A implies B and B implies C.
rules.fix_template: Replace symbolic quantifiers/implications with words in prose; keep symbols inside a displayed formula if needed.

rules.title: Keep the reader oriented about goals and structure
rules.rationale: Readers need reminders of what is being proved and why steps are taken.
rules.check: Flag long proof stretches (>10 lines/paragraphs) without a stated local goal or without referencing the theorem statement.
rules.pass_criteria: Proof includes goal statements at major transitions (e.g., “It remains to show…”, “We now prove…”).
exceptions: Very short proofs where the goal is obvious from immediate context.
Highly routine calculations with explicit displayed derivations.
bad_examples: Several paragraphs of computation with no statement of what is being established.
good_examples: To conclude the theorem, it suffices to show that the error term tends to zero.
rules.fix_template: Add a one-sentence roadmap at transition points describing the subgoal and how it connects to the main claim.

rules.title: Use complete English sentences around math
rules.rationale: Sentence fragments around formulas introduce ambiguity and grammatical breaks.
rules.check: Flag fragments like “Since …” followed by a display with no main verb, or displays introduced by incorrect punctuation (e.g., random colon).
rules.pass_criteria: Each paragraph reads as grammatical English when formulas are read aloud.
exceptions: Bulleted lists explicitly labeled as fragments.
Caption-style text in figures/tables.
bad_examples:
If C and P are subsets of N^n, let:
  L(C,P)=...
good_examples: Since x>0, we have x^2>0.
If C and P are subsets of N^n, let L(C,P) denote ...
rules.fix_template: Rewrite fragments into full sentences; adjust punctuation so the display is a grammatical clause of the sentence.

rules.title: Capitalize 'Theorem', 'Lemma', etc. when used as labeled nouns
rules.rationale: Consistent capitalization improves professional readability and aligns with many editorial norms.
rules.check: Flag patterns like “theorem 2” or “lemma 5” when they refer to a labeled result.
rules.pass_criteria: Use “Theorem 2”, “Lemma 5”, “Proposition 3.1” when referring to numbered items.
exceptions: Generic usage: “a theorem of algebra” is not capitalized.
good_examples: As shown in Theorem 2, ...
Lemma 5 implies ...
rules.fix_template: Capitalize the noun when it refers to a labeled result (Theorem/Lemma/Proposition/Corollary/Definition).

rules.title: Avoid gratuitous colons before displays
rules.rationale: Colons are often misused before displayed equations and can create ungrammatical sentences.
rules.check: Flag occurrences like “We have:” or “Let:” immediately before a display when the display merely completes the clause.
rules.pass_criteria: Use a colon only when introducing a list or explanation; otherwise treat the display as a continuation without punctuation after forms of 'to be'.
exceptions: Legitimate list introductions: “as follows:” followed by a list of items.
Algorithm blocks introduced by “Algorithm:” in some venues.
bad_examples:
If C and P are subsets of N^n, let:
  L(C,P)=...
good_examples:
We have
  x = y + 1.

If C and P are subsets of N^n, let L(C,P) be defined by
  L(C,P)=...
rules.fix_template: Remove the colon and rewrite so the displayed equation completes a grammatical sentence.

rules.title: Minimize symbol-heavy prose when words are clearer
rules.rationale: Symbol-dense prose is cognitively expensive and increases scope ambiguity.
rules.check: Flag passages with many quantifier/logic symbols in running text outside displayed formulas.
rules.pass_criteria: Use words for quantifiers and logical connectors in prose; reserve dense notation for displays.
exceptions: Formal logic, proof theory, or papers where symbolic logic is the object of study.
good_examples: For every ε>0 there exists δ>0 such that if |x-a|<δ then |f(x)-f(a)|<ε.
rules.fix_template: Move the dense symbolic statement into a display or rewrite it in words in running text.

embedded.title: IEEE and ACM Citation Rules (LLM Audit Rules)
embedded.description: Practical checks to validate IEEE and ACM reference/citation conformance: numeric bracket citations, ordering/numbering constraints, DOI/URL expectations, and prose conventions around citations.

sources.source_name: IEEE Editorial Style Manual for Authors
IEEE template instructions on references and citations
ACM Citation Style and Reference Formats

rules.title: Every in-text citation must have a matching reference entry
rules.rationale: Missing bibliography entries break traceability and can be grounds for rejection or major revision.
rules.check: Flag any in-text numeric citation (e.g., [12]) whose number is not present as a leading reference label in the reference list.
rules.pass_criteria: All in-text citation numbers correspond to an entry in the reference list.
exceptions: Draft manuscripts where references are intentionally omitted (must be explicitly indicated).
rules.fix_template: Add the missing reference entry or remove/replace the in-text citation.

rules.title: Every reference entry must be cited in the text
rules.rationale: Uncited references are often disallowed or indicate sloppy citation management.
rules.check: Flag reference-list entries whose index never appears as an in-text citation.
rules.pass_criteria: All reference entries are cited at least once in the body, captions, or appendices as allowed by the venue.
exceptions: Bibliographies used as background reading lists (rare; must be labeled as such).
rules.fix_template: Either cite the reference where relevant or remove it from the reference list.

rules.title: IEEE uses one reference number per distinct reference entry
rules.rationale: IEEE forbids grouping multiple references under one reference number.
rules.check: Flag any single reference number that contains multiple independent sources separated by semicolons or multiple publisher/year patterns.
rules.pass_criteria: Each distinct source has its own reference number entry.
exceptions: One source with multiple parts/chapters is cited as one entry with in-text pinpoint locators.
rules.fix_template: Split the grouped reference into multiple numbered entries and renumber citations accordingly.

rules.title: Do not write 'in reference [n]' mid-sentence; use 'in [n]'
rules.rationale: IEEE editorial guidance prefers concise numeric citation prose.
rules.check: Flag phrases matching /in\s+reference\s+\[\d+\]/ or /in\s+Ref\.?\s*\[\d+\]/.
rules.pass_criteria: Use the form “in [n] …” (and omit author name with the bracket number unless necessary).
exceptions: If the sentence begins with “Reference [n] ...” and cannot be rewritten.
If author name is integral to meaning (rare).
rules.fix_template: Rewrite the clause so it reads “in [n] …” or restructure the sentence to avoid “reference”.

rules.title: Use IEEE cross-reference notation for pinpointing figures/equations/sections
rules.rationale: IEEE specifies bracketed cross-reference notation such as [1, Fig. 2] and [1, eq. (8)].
rules.check: Flag phrases like “Fig. 2 of reference [n]” or “equation (8) of reference [n]”.
rules.pass_criteria: Use IEEE cross-reference notation: [n, Fig. 2], [n, eq. (8)], [n, Sec. IV], etc.
exceptions: Venues within IEEE that explicitly override this detail (rare).
rules.fix_template: Replace with the IEEE bracket cross-reference format, e.g., “in [n, Fig. 2]”.

rules.title: Do not allocate multiple reference numbers to different parts of the same source
rules.rationale: IEEE prefers a single reference entry for a source, with in-text pinpointing for pages/sections/etc.
rules.check: Flag repeated identical author/title/year sources appearing as separate reference-list entries with different numbers.
rules.pass_criteria: One source appears once in the reference list; in-text cites use locators like [n, Ch. 3] as needed.
exceptions: Materially distinct editions or translations; then treat as distinct sources.
rules.fix_template: Merge duplicate entries into one and update in-text citations to pinpoint the relevant location.

rules.title: Reference numbering should match IEEE numeric system conventions
rules.rationale: IEEE systems typically expect stable numeric numbering; renumbering needs consistency.
rules.check: Flag inconsistent or non-numeric reference labels (e.g., [A], [B]) unless the venue profile allows it.
rules.pass_criteria: References are numbered with Arabic numerals in brackets.
exceptions: Venue-specific overrides (e.g., magazine formats).
rules.fix_template: Convert reference labels to numeric bracketed form consistent across text and reference list.

rules.title: ACM in-text citations are numeric in square brackets
rules.rationale: ACM's reference-formatting guidance specifies numeric bracketed citations (e.g., [1]).
rules.check: Flag non-numeric citation systems (author-year parentheses, footnote-only citations) when ACM profile is selected.
rules.pass_criteria: In-text citations appear as [n] or [n, m] for multiple citations.
exceptions: acmart author-year mode explicitly enabled (then check author-year format instead).
rules.fix_template: Switch to ACM numeric citation mode or enable and consistently use acmart author-year mode.

rules.title: Use ACM Reference Format; nonconforming references may be returned
rules.rationale: ACM explicitly warns that papers may be returned for proper formatting if references do not adhere.
rules.check: Flag mixed reference styles (e.g., APA-like entries) that deviate from ACM examples, especially missing required fields like venue, pages, and DOI when available.
rules.pass_criteria: Reference list matches ACM Reference Format for each entry type.
exceptions: Pre-submission drafts where references are not yet finalized (must be marked).
rules.fix_template: Regenerate the bibliography using ACM-Reference-Format.bst (BibTeX) or the ACM BibLaTeX setup and correct missing fields.

rules.title: Prefer DOIs over URLs when both exist
rules.rationale: ACM guidance prefers DOIs because URLs are not permanent.
rules.check: Flag references that include only a URL when a DOI is known or easily discoverable (common for journal/conference papers).
rules.pass_criteria: If a DOI exists, include it; suppress raw URLs unless a distinct URL is required.
exceptions: No DOI exists (web pages, some technical reports).
A distinct URL is needed in addition to DOI (use distinctURL/archived mechanisms where supported).
rules.fix_template: Add the DOI field; if needed, keep URL with lastaccessed/archived metadata.

rules.title: For web sources, include access date and archival link when feasible
rules.rationale: acmart supports lastaccessed and archived fields to mitigate stale links and content drift.
rules.check: Flag @online or URL-only references without lastaccessed date; flag missing archived links for unstable web content.
rules.pass_criteria: Web resources include lastaccessed and, when available, an archived snapshot link.
exceptions: Stable DOI-resolvable resources.
Publisher-hosted PDFs with long-term stability guarantees (still optional).
rules.fix_template: Add lastaccessed = {Month Day, Year} and archived = {Wayback snapshot URL} when possible.

rules.title: ACM prefers full author names (not initials) in references
rules.rationale: ACM explicitly prefers full names to facilitate indexing and citation linking.
rules.check: Flag references where authors are given only as initials when ACM examples show full names.
rules.pass_criteria: Author names are listed as full given names where available (BibTeX entries should include them).
exceptions: Authors who publish under initials or have unknown given names.
Legacy metadata where full names are unavailable.
rules.fix_template: Replace initials with full given names as in ACM examples or ensure the .bib metadata contains full names.

rules.title: acmart numeric citations are the default; ensure consistency with selected mode
rules.rationale: acmart supports both numeric and author-year; inconsistent use leads to processing delays.
rules.check: Flag mixed citation modes (some [n], some (Author, Year)) in the same manuscript.
rules.pass_criteria: Exactly one citation mode is used consistently across the paper.
exceptions: Explicitly permitted hybrid conventions by a specific ACM venue (rare).
rules.fix_template: Choose either numeric (default) or author-year (\citestyle{acmauthoryear}) and regenerate the bibliography.

embedded.title: Strict Theorem-and-Proof Rigor Audit Rules
embedded.description: Strict rules for mathematical correctness auditing: hypotheses, quantifiers, scope, proof structure, justification, case completeness, and conclusion matching.

provenance.basis: Curated engineering rules for formal math auditing; similar in spirit to proof-checking checklists, but aimed at mathematical validity.

rules.title: State all hypotheses explicitly
rules.check: Flag theorem statements whose conclusions rely on unstated assumptions: topology, boundary conditions, initialization, or parameter ranges.
rules.pass_criteria: All assumptions needed by the proof are stated in the theorem/lemma statement, the local setup, or an explicitly inherited standing assumptions block.
failure_modes: Regularity used later but absent from the statement
rules.fix_template: Add the missing assumptions to the theorem statement or explicitly cite standing assumptions.

rules.title: Quantifiers and domains must be explicit
rules.check: Flag free variables, ambiguous quantifier order, or missing domains in theorem statements and proof steps.
rules.pass_criteria: Every variable is bound or declared, and quantifier order matches the intended claim.
rules.fix_template: Rewrite the statement to make all quantifiers and domains explicit (e.g., “for all x∈X” / “there exists δ>0”).

rules.title: No undefined objects inside proofs
rules.check: Flag objects introduced without definition: operators, norms, measures, constants, or sets.
rules.pass_criteria: Each object introduced is defined with enough detail to make subsequent steps checkable.
failure_modes: Using an operator T without specifying its domain/codomain
Introducing a constant C without stating dependence
rules.fix_template: Define the object when introduced (domain/codomain/assumptions), or cite where it was defined.

rules.title: Proof must close exactly the stated claim
rules.check: Compare the final line of the proof to the theorem claim and flag partial, stronger, weaker, or shifted conclusions.
rules.pass_criteria: The proof closes the exact theorem claim under the stated assumptions.
failure_modes: Special case proved instead of general claim
rules.fix_template: Either strengthen the proof or weaken the theorem statement to what is actually proved.

rules.title: Every nontrivial inference step must be justified
rules.check: Flag leaps using therefore, hence, thus, implies, or 'it follows' where justification is absent and not standard from the immediately preceding line.
rules.pass_criteria: Each nontrivial step cites a theorem, lemma, definition, inequality, algebraic manipulation, or clearly checkable argument.
failure_modes: Exchange of limit and integral with no theorem
rules.fix_template: Add the missing justification or split the leap into smaller verified steps.

rules.title: Avoid variable capture and scope errors
rules.check: Flag reuse of the same symbol for different instantiated objects within overlapping scope; flag variables that escape their quantifier scope.
rules.pass_criteria: Bound variables remain local; newly instantiated objects get fresh names; reused dummy indices are confined to local formulas.
failure_modes: Using k for two different existentials
Treating a bound variable as globally fixed
rules.fix_template: Rename variables to avoid clashes and restate the instantiation step explicitly.

rules.title: Existence proofs must not assume the object being proved
rules.check: Flag “let x be such that …” when the existence of x is the conclusion, not a hypothesis.
rules.pass_criteria: Existence is established by construction or by citing an existence theorem; then the object is named.
rules.fix_template: Either construct the object or cite the theorem that guarantees existence before introducing the symbol.

rules.title: Case splits must be exhaustive and clearly structured
rules.check: Flag proofs that say “consider cases” without stating all cases or without showing they cover all possibilities.
rules.pass_criteria: Cases cover the entire domain and are either disjoint or overlap handled explicitly.
failure_modes: Case structure not connected to the goal
rules.fix_template: List the cases explicitly and add a one-line argument that they cover all possibilities.

rules.title: Induction proofs must include base case and induction step
rules.check: Flag induction proofs missing base case(s), missing induction hypothesis, or unclear induction variable domain.
rules.pass_criteria: Base case(s) verified; induction hypothesis stated; induction step proved; conclusion applies to intended domain.
failure_modes: Strong induction used without stating range
rules.fix_template: Add explicit base case, IH, and step; clarify whether induction is weak/strong and the starting index.

rules.title: Proof by contradiction must exhibit a clear contradiction
rules.check: Flag contradiction proofs that never state an explicit false statement or that end with 'contradiction' without identifying it.
rules.pass_criteria: The negation is assumed; a concrete contradiction (C and not C, or an explicitly false inequality) is derived.
failure_modes: Contradiction is asserted but not shown
rules.fix_template: State the assumed negation explicitly and identify the precise contradictory statements at the end.

rules.title: Iff / equivalence proofs must prove both directions
rules.check: Flag claims of equivalence with only one implication proved or the reverse direction missing.
rules.pass_criteria: Both P⇒Q and Q⇒P are proved (or an equivalent characterization is established).
rules.fix_template: Add the missing direction in a separate paragraph or restructure as two implications.

rules.title: Avoid 'obvious/clearly/trivial' without explanation
rules.check: Flag words: obvious, clearly, trivial, immediate, evident when used to replace reasoning.
rules.pass_criteria: If such words are used, a short reason follows, or the word is removed.
exceptions: Very standard algebraic step with a cited lemma or definition
Venue where such phrasing is explicitly acceptable (still discouraged)
rules.fix_template: Delete the word or add a short justification sentence (1–2 lines).

rules.title: Proof should indicate where hypotheses are used
rules.check: Flag long proofs where assumptions appear in statement but are never referenced or used in reasoning.
rules.pass_criteria: Major assumptions are invoked at least once in the proof, or explicitly stated as unused/redundant.
failure_modes: Hypothesis used implicitly but never mentioned
rules.fix_template: Insert a sentence indicating where each key hypothesis is applied, or remove redundant hypotheses.

rules.title: No silent strengthening/weakening of quantifiers in proof
rules.check: Flag steps that change “for all” to “for some”, or replace arbitrary element with a special one without justification.
rules.pass_criteria: Quantifier changes are explicitly justified (e.g., choose a generic element, then fix it).
failure_modes: Choosing a convenient x without loss justification
Proving existence for a specific parameter rather than arbitrary
rules.fix_template: Restate the quantifier structure and ensure the proof introduces elements in the correct order (generic then specific).

rules.title: Equality/inequality chains must be logically annotated when nontrivial
rules.check: Flag long chains of equalities/inequalities without stating which theorem or property justifies each step, especially when steps are not algebraic identities.
rules.pass_criteria: Nontrivial steps cite the reason (monotonicity, Jensen, triangle inequality, dominated convergence, etc.).
failure_modes: Measure-theoretic limit step with no condition
rules.fix_template: Break the chain and annotate each nontrivial transformation with the justification.

rules.title: Definitions must precede the theorems that use them
rules.check: Flag theorems using a defined term that has not been defined yet in the document order.
rules.pass_criteria: All nonstandard terms are defined before first usage in theorem statements; if deferred, a Notation/Preliminaries section is referenced explicitly.
rules.fix_template: Move the definition earlier or add a forward reference to the Notation section and ensure it exists.

rules.title: Uniqueness language requires proof of uniqueness
rules.check: Flag usage of “the solution”, “the minimizer”, “the fixed point” when uniqueness has not been proved.
rules.pass_criteria: Use “a” unless uniqueness is established or cited.
failure_modes: Referring to 'the optimal solution' without uniqueness
rules.fix_template: Change “the” → “an” or add a uniqueness argument/citation.

rules.title: Avoid mixing informal commentary into the proof body
rules.check: Flag paragraphs inside proofs that indicate motivation/handwaving without labeling (e.g., “intuitively”, “we guess”, “it seems”).
rules.pass_criteria: Clearly separate rigorous argument from commentary via 'Remark', 'Idea of proof', or explicit labels.
exceptions: Proof sketch sections clearly labeled as sketches.
rules.fix_template: Move commentary into a Remark/Discussion or label it explicitly as a sketch; keep the proof body rigorous.
